text: Adding maximal strength and plyometric training twice weekly for 10 weeks improves running economy durability during prolonged running at marathon intensity in well-trained male runners.
Supplementary strength training improved running economy at 90 minutes of heavy-intensity running by 2.1% compared to a 0.6% worsening in the running-only control group.
Strength training increased fatigued high-intensity time to exhaustion by 35% after a 90-minute heavy-intensity run, while the running-only group declined by 8%.
Strength training benefits running economy more in a fatigued state than in a fresh state, suggesting RE durability is a distinct and trainable quality.

Maximal strength and plyometric training can improve a runner's ability to sustain high-intensity effort in the final stages of a race, simulated by a time-to-exhaustion test at 95% VO2max after prolonged running.
Running at approximately 80% VO2max (heavy-intensity domain, between lactate threshold 1 and 2) for 90 minutes causes progressive worsening of running economy in runners who do not strength train.
